html:
[406]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 觀此而哀小民之愚也 - Observing this, one grieves for the foolishness of the common people. Sets a tone of pity and moral judgment.
  • 相率而為非 - To follow one another into wrongdoing. Suggests a collective moral failure.
  • 朋黨所要 - By the demands of factions. Indicates the influence of group pressure or political factions.
  • 橫行而不顧 - To act recklessly without regard. Conveys a sense of lawlessness and disregard for consequences.
  • 自罹法網 - To fall into the legal net. Metaphorically describes being caught by the law.
  • 幸脫無由 - Without a way to escape. Emphasizes the inescapable nature of one's fate.
  • 猶矜奔避 - Still they boast of fleeing. Suggests a futile attempt at evasion or denial.
  • 真乃醉生夢死 - Truly they are living in a drunken, dreamlike state. Metaphorically describes a state of moral and intellectual numbness.
  • 誰能唤醒其良心 - Who can awaken their conscience? Rhetorical question underscores the moral blindness of the people.
  • 為鬼為人 - Whether as ghosts or humans. Suggests a blurred moral boundary.
  • 尚未辨别其形似者也 - They have not yet distinguished their true nature. Indicates a lack of self-awareness or moral clarity.
  • 尚其念氓蚩之可悯 - Remember that the common people are pitiable. Moral exhortation to the rulers.
  • 勿以罔民而可为 - Do not consider deceiving the people as acceptable. Warns against exploiting the people.
  • 思人性之皆良 - Consider that human nature is inherently good. Reflects a Confucian belief in the potential for moral improvement.
  • 勿致不教而遂杀 - Do not allow them to be killed without proper education. Emphasizes the importance of moral instruction.
  • 被泽者，可胜计哉 - Those who benefit from this can be counted in the millions. Suggests the widespread positive impact of proper governance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蘭岩 - A name, likely referring to a person or a literary figure, but without specific historical context.
  • 小民 - Refers to the common people or peasants, a common subject in classical Chinese texts.
  • 氓蚩 - Refers to the common people, often used in a derogatory sense to describe their perceived ignorance or foolishness.
  • 法網 - A metaphor for the law or legal system, often used in a negative sense to describe its harshness or inescapability.
  • 良心 - Refers to conscience or moral sense, a key concept in Confucian ethics.
  • 為民父母 - A metaphor for rulers or officials who are supposed to care for the people like parents.
  • 被澤 - Refers to those who benefit from the ruler's benevolence or policies.

Proper Nouns

  • 蘭岩 - A name, likely referring to a person or a literary figure, but without specific historical context.
  • 谭九 - A name, likely referring to a person, but without specific historical context.
  • 京都 - Refers to the capital city, likely referring to the imperial capital of the time.
  • 烟郊 - Refers to a specific location, likely a suburb or outskirts of the capital.

[407]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 室中空无所有 - Literally 'the room is empty of all things,' emphasizing the sparseness of the room.
  • 惟篝灯悬壁 - 'Only a lamp hanging on the wall,' highlighting the minimal lighting.
  • 一少妇卧炕头哺儿 - 'A young woman lying on the kang (heating platform) feeding her child,' describing the scene with a focus on the mother and child.
  • 徐徐起 - 'Slowly gets up,' indicating the woman's deliberate and perhaps weary movement.
  • 掠鬓 - 'Combing her hair,' a gentle action that adds to the description of her movements.
  • 呱呱啼 - 'Crying loudly,' a vivid depiction of the baby's cry.
  • 探袖出胡饼一枚 - 'Reaches into her sleeve and takes out a round flatbread,' showing a specific action and object.
  • 泪睫惨黛 - 'Her eyelashes are wet and her eyebrows are sad,' a poetic way to describe her sorrowful appearance.
  • 殊少欢容 - 'Lacks a joyful expression,' reinforcing her sad demeanor.
  • 折穄引火于灯 - 'Breaks some millet and uses it to light the lamp,' a specific and traditional method of lighting.
  • 着红布短袄，绿布裤，蓝布短袜，趿高底破红鞋 - 'Wearing a red cotton jacket, green cotton pants, blue cotton socks, and dragging along a pair of high-heeled, red, worn-out shoes,' a detailed description of her attire.
  • 皆敝甚 - 'All very worn out,' emphasizing the poor condition of her clothes.
  • 露一肘一腓并两踵焉 - 'Revealing one elbow, one calf, and both heels,' a vivid description of her disheveled state.
  • 年少口讷 - 'Young and tongue-tied,' describing the narrator's character.
  • 不能致诘 - 'Unable to ask questions,' indicating the narrator's shyness.
  • 但阴怜之 - 'But secretly feeling sympathy for her,' expressing the narrator's inner thoughts.
  • 为还代步 - 'To return the horse,' indicating the purpose of the old woman's action.
  • 致郎寂坐 - 'To make the guest sit alone,' a polite way of saying the guest is left alone.
  • 渠宅上闻有客至，亦欲延款 - 'Her house heard that a guest had arrived, and they also wanted to entertain,' showing the old woman's explanation for her actions.
  • 老身辞以太晚 - 'I declined due to it being too late,' a polite refusal.
  • 嘱为致意 - 'Told me to convey greetings,' indicating a request for the narrator to pass on a message.
  • 奔驰半日 - 'Traveled for half a day,' emphasizing the duration of travel.
  • 想客亦苦饥矣 - 'I imagine the guest must be very hungry by now,' a sympathetic statement.
  • 媳妇备饭来 - 'The daughter-in-law will prepare the meal,' indicating the next action.
  • 老身且出喂驴 - 'I will go out to feed the donkey,' a polite way of saying she will leave.
  • 相扰何安 - 'How can I trouble you,' a polite expression of gratitude.
  • 刍豆之费，临行当厚偿 - 'The cost of hay and beans, I will pay generously when I leave,' a promise of compensation.
  • 莫漫作客套语 - 'Don't just speak in formalities,' a request for sincerity.
  • 所值几何哉 - 'How much is it worth anyway,' a rhetorical question indicating the value is minimal.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 炕头 - A traditional heating platform used in northern China, often used as a bed.
  • 胡饼 - A type of flatbread, possibly a reference to a traditional food.
  • 高底破红鞋 - High-heeled, red, worn-out shoes, which might be a traditional or specific type of footwear.
  • 老身 - A respectful term for an elderly woman, often used by older women to refer to themselves.
  • 媳妇 - Daughter-in-law, a term used to refer to a wife in a traditional family setting.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes the room and its contents, with a focus on the minimal setup and the mother feeding her child.
  • This sequence of actions describes the woman's movements and the baby's reaction.
  • This shows the cause-and-effect relationship between the woman's action and the baby's behavior.
  • This describes the woman's emotional state, emphasizing her sadness.
  • This detailed description of her actions and attire provides a vivid picture of her condition.
  • This emphasizes the poor condition of her clothes, adding to the overall image of her poverty.
  • This describes the narrator's character and his feelings towards the woman.
  • This explains the old woman's actions and the narrator's situation.
  • This provides the old woman's explanation for her actions and her request to the narrator.
  • This describes the narrator's travel and his concern for the guest's well-being.
  • This shows the sequence of events and the old woman's departure.
  • This expresses the narrator's gratitude and his promise to pay.
  • This requests sincerity and downplays the value of the services provided.

[408]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 婦陈列酒肴 - Classical phrase meaning 'the woman set out wine and dishes.' Indicates a hostess preparing a meal, though the quality is described as poor.
  • 瓦器绝粗 - 'Clay vessels were extremely coarse,' indicating the low quality of the utensils used.
  • 折稊为箸 - 'Bent reeds into chopsticks,' a humorous or ironic way of describing makeshift dining utensils.
  • 以盆代壶 - 'Used a basin in place of a pot,' another example of makeshift or improvised tableware.
  • 冷不中啖 - 'Cold and unfit to eat,' indicating that the food was not only cold but also unpalatable.
  • 敛具去 - 'Put away the utensils and left,' a concise way of describing the conclusion of a meal.
  • 就灯为儿捉虱 - 'By the lamp, she caught lice on her child,' a vivid but humble image of domestic life.
  • 听姥言似非京师人 - 'From listening to your words, it seems you are not from the capital,' a polite way of inquiring about someone’s background.
  • 娘子则又旗妆 - 'But your wife is dressed in a banner-style costume,' indicating a woman dressed in a style associated with military or ceremonial attire.
  • 敢问邦旗 - 'I dare to ask about your homeland and banners,' a formal and respectful way to ask about one’s origin and social status.
  • 诚如郎说 - 'Indeed as you said,' a direct way of agreeing with someone.
  • 身本凤阳侯氏 - 'Originally from the House of Fengyang Hou,' indicating noble ancestry.
  • 因岁荒流离入京 - 'Because of a bad harvest, we were displaced and came to the capital,' a common expression for migration due to famine.
  • 为人缝纫补辍 - 'I sewed and mended for people,' indicating a humble occupation.
  • 谋衣食 - 'To secure food and clothing,' a common phrase for earning a living.
  • 再醮此间村民郝四 - 'I remarried to a villager named Hao Si here,' a formal way of stating a second marriage.
  • 近三十年 - 'Nearly thirty years ago,' indicating the length of time since an event.
  • 今成翁矣 - 'Now he is an old man,' a respectful way of referring to an elderly husband.
  • 生一女一子 - 'Had one daughter and one son,' a standard way of listing children.
  • 女已适人 - 'The daughter has married,' a traditional expression.
  • 子为圬者 - 'The son is a plasterer,' indicating a trade.
  • 居城中 - 'Lives in the city,' indicating the son's current residence.
  • 翁以衰耄 - 'The old man, now aged and infirm,' a respectful way of referring to an elderly husband.
  • 佣于野肆中 - 'Works as a laborer in a roadside tavern,' indicating a menial job.
  • 为人提壶涤器 - 'He carries pots and cleans vessels for others,' a description of the husband’s work.
  • 小郎明日当过其处 - 'The young master will pass by there tomorrow,' a polite way of referring to the son.
  • 鸡皮白髭 - 'Chicken-skin and white beard,' a vivid description of an old man.
  • 耳后有瘤如卵大者 - 'A lump the size of an egg behind the ear,' a distinctive physical feature.
  • 媳妇余氏 - 'My daughter-in-law, Yu,' a respectful way of referring to one’s daughter-in-law.
  • 实宅上婢子 - 'Actually, she was a servant in the house,' indicating the daughter-in-law’s previous status.
  • 其主人为巴参领 - 'Her former master was the Ba Commandant,' indicating the position of the former employer.
  • 久退闲 - 'Has long since retired,' indicating the former employer is no longer in office.
  • 幼主袭职矣 - 'The young master has inherited the position,' a respectful way of referring to a successor.
  • 适借马处也 - 'This is just a place where we borrowed a horse,' a modest way of explaining the location.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 京师 - Refers to the capital city, likely Beijing or Nanjing depending on the historical context, but generally denotes the imperial capital.
  • 凤阳侯氏 - Indicates a noble family from Fengyang, a region in Anhui province, historically associated with the Ming dynasty’s imperial family.
  • 中元节 - The 'Ghost Festival,' a traditional Chinese festival held on the 15th day of the 7th lunar month, during which people honor the dead.
  • 分得宅上余 - 'Received a share from the manor’s leftovers,' referring to the distribution of food or resources from a noble estate during festivals.
  • 亵渎 - 'To desecrate' or 'to treat with disrespect,' used here in the sense of feeling unworthy to receive such food.
  • 盛设待客 - 'A lavish setup for entertaining guests,' a polite term for hosting.
  • 再醮 - A term used to describe a woman who has remarried, often with connotations of social stigma or transition.
  • 圬者 - A 'plasterer' or 'mason,' indicating a working-class trade.
  • 野肆 - A 'country tavern' or 'roadside inn,' often associated with lower-class or traveling workers.
  • 参领 - A military or administrative title, likely referring to a mid-level official or commander.
  • 袭职 - 'Inheriting a position,' a common term for passing down a family or official title.

Proper Nouns

  • 谭 - The name of the male character, likely a guest or visitor.
  • 媪 - The old woman, a respectful term for an elderly woman.
  • 凤阳侯氏 - A noble family from Fengyang.
  • 郝四 - A male name, likely the husband of the old woman.
  • 余氏 - The surname of the daughter-in-law.

[409]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 坐久颇倦 - Literally 'sitting for a long time and feeling tired,' a common expression to describe fatigue after prolonged sitting.
  • 不便偃息 - Literally 'not convenient to lie down,' indicating a physical discomfort or awkwardness that prevents lying down.
  • 具就灯吸烟 - Literally 'bring out and smoke by the lamp,' a colloquial phrase describing the act of lighting up a cigarette.
  • 频睃 - Literally 'frequently glance,' indicating a woman's repeated, possibly longing, glances.
  • 有欲烟之色 - Literally 'with the look of wanting to smoke,' describing a woman's desire for a cigarette.
  • 察知其意 - Literally 'perceive her intention,' indicating the grandmother's awareness of the woman's desire.
  • 拊掌曰 - Literally 'clap hands and say,' a gesture indicating excitement or agreement.
  • 媳妇垂诞吃烟矣 - Literally 'the daughter-in-law is drooling over smoking,' a vivid expression of strong desire.
  • 小郎肯见赐否 - Literally 'would the young master be so kind as to give it,' a polite way of asking for something.
  • 烟囊付之 - Literally 'hand over the cigarette pouch,' indicating the act of giving the cigarette pouch.
  • 窘迫 - Literally 'in a difficult situation,' indicating financial or situational hardship.
  • 不有此物已半年矣 - Literally 'hasn't had this thing for half a year,' indicating a long absence of a particular item.
  • 有烟具 - Literally 'have a smoking device,' indicating the possession of a smoking accessory.
  • 吸之甚适 - Literally 'smoke it very comfortably,' indicating a pleasant experience.
  • 眉颦顿舒 - Literally 'frown suddenly relaxes,' indicating a change from worry to relief or pleasure.
  • 老身在世六十余年 - Literally 'I have been in this world for over sixty years,' indicating the grandmother's age.
  • 不识此味 - Literally 'do not know this taste,' indicating unfamiliarity with the experience.
  • 嗜痂者 - Literally 'those who like scabs,' a metaphor for people who enjoy something others find repulsive.
  • 何故好之如此 - Literally 'why do they like it so much,' expressing curiosity about a strong preference.
  • 亦自不解 - Literally 'also do not understand myself,' indicating self-ignorance or confusion.
  • 第不会则已 - Literally 'if one does not understand, it is fine,' indicating acceptance of ignorance.
  • 学会则一刻不能离 - Literally 'once learned, one cannot be away for a moment,' indicating a strong dependency.
  • 宁可食无饭 - Literally 'rather than eat no rice,' indicating a preference for smoking over eating.
  • 不可吸无烟也 - Literally 'cannot smoke without tobacco,' indicating the necessity of smoking.
  • 娘子嗜此 - Literally 'the lady likes this,' indicating a woman's fondness for something.
  • 迟日当市具与烟来 - Literally 'on a slow day, I will buy the device and tobacco and come,' indicating a future action.
  • 作野人芹敬 - Literally 'as a humble offering from a rustic,' indicating a modest gift.
  • 颔之 - Literally 'nod in agreement,' indicating approval or assent.

Proper Nouns

  • 谭 - The name of the man, a proper noun.
  • 媪 - The name of the grandmother, a proper noun.
  • 媳妇 - The name of the woman, a proper noun.

[410]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 出溲 - Classical phrase meaning 'to relieve oneself,' often used in a polite or euphemistic sense.
  • 银河西耿 - Literally 'the Milky Way shining westward,' describing the night sky with the Milky Way visible in the west.
  • 斜月在林 - Describes a slanted moon in the trees, creating a serene and picturesque scene.
  • 约略四更 - An approximate time, meaning around the fourth watch of the night (approximately 2-3 AM).
  • 客不时欠伸 - Describes a guest yawning or stretching, indicating fatigue or discomfort.
  • 寝息 - Means 'to rest' or 'to sleep.'
  • 尚可稍坐 - A polite refusal, meaning 'I can still sit for a while.'
  • 勿太勉强 - Advises against forcing oneself, meaning 'don't push it too hard.'
  • 明日尚有路行 - Indicates that there is still a journey ahead the next day.
  • 有所恳 - Means 'a request' or 'a favor.'
  • 留意 - Means 'pay attention' or 'keep in mind.'
  • 我家老翁 - Refers to the woman's husband or father.
  • 促其急送数钱来 - Urges someone to quickly send some money.
  • 家中吃着都尽矣 - Means 'the food and money in the house are all gone.'
  • 假一席地 - Means 'to borrow a place to rest.'
  • 得一夕安 - Means 'to get a peaceful night's rest.'
  • 已承厚贶 - Means 'I have already received a great favor.'
  • 敢过望耶 - A polite way of saying 'I wouldn't dare to ask for more.'
  • 各就枕 - Means 'each went to bed.'
  • 着枕便熟睡 - Means 'fell asleep immediately upon lying down.'
  • 梦回 - Means 'woke up from a dream.'
  • 觉草虫鸣于耳畔 - Describes hearing the sound of grasshoppers or crickets near the ear.
  • 荧火耀于目前 - Describes seeing the light of fireflies.
  • 矍然惊起 - Means 'suddenly woke up in alarm.'
  • 身卧松柏间 - Describes lying in a pine or cypress forest.
  • 秋露湿衣 - Means 'autumn dew wet my clothes.'
  • 清寒砭骨 - Means 'the cold penetrates the bones.'
  • 系驴树根上 - Means 'tied the donkey to the tree roots.'
  • 龁草不休 - Means 'chewing grass continuously.'
  • 茅舍乌有 - Means 'the thatched cottage was nowhere to be found.'
  • 媪与妇并失所在 - Means 'the old woman and the young woman were both gone.'
  • 古冢颓然 - Describes an ancient tomb in ruins.
  • 半倾于蒿莱枳棘之中 - Means 'half-tilted among wild grass and thorns.'
  • 毛发森竖 - Means 'hair stood on end with fear.'
  • 急捉驴乘之 - Means 'quickly grabbed the donkey and mounted it.'
  • 得得而驱 - Means 'rode away steadily.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 银河西耿，斜月在林 - Describes a typical Chinese poetic scene of a night sky with the Milky Way and a slanted moon, often used to evoke a sense of tranquility and beauty.
  • 四更 - Refers to the fourth watch of the night, which is approximately 2-3 AM in the traditional Chinese time system.
  • 媪 - Refers to an old woman, often used in classical literature to describe a maternal or nurturing figure.
  • 妇 - Refers to a young woman, often used in classical literature to describe a wife or daughter.
  • 老翁 - Refers to an elderly man, often used to describe a husband or father.
  • 茅舍 - Refers to a thatched cottage, a common type of rural dwelling in ancient China.
  • 古冢 - Refers to an ancient tomb, often used to evoke a sense of history and mystery.
  • 蒿莱枳棘 - Refers to wild grass and thorns, often used to describe a desolate or abandoned place.

Proper Nouns

  • 谭 - The name of the narrator.
  • 媪 - The old woman.
  • 妇 - The young woman.
  • 老翁 - The old man (husband or father of the old woman).

[411]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 行三五里，天已向曙 - Descriptive phrase indicating that after walking three to five miles, the sky begins to lighten, signaling dawn.
  • 稍稍心定 - Means 'gradually calmed down,' describing a state of mind becoming more composed.
  • 抵烟郊事毕 - Means 'reached the outskirts of the town and finished the business,' indicating the completion of a task in a specific location.
  • 复遵故道 - Means 'returned along the old path,' suggesting a retracing of one's steps.
  • 小歇旗亭 - Means 'took a short rest at a flag pavilion,' where a flag pavilion is a place for travelers to rest or refresh themselves.
  • 酷肖老媪所述 - Means 'very much resembled what the old woman had described,' indicating a strong similarity.
  • 泫然曰 - Means 'said with tears in his eyes,' describing an emotional state.
  • 讵意尚聚首地下哉 - Means 'who would have thought they were still together in the underworld,' expressing surprise and disbelief.
  • 谭亦恻然 - Means 'Tan also felt moved,' describing an emotional response.
  • 更问巴参领为何如人 - Means 'then asked what kind of person Ba was,' indicating a further inquiry.
  • 直北乔木处 - Means 'directly north where there are tall trees,' providing a geographical description.
  • 即其墓道 - Means 'which is the path to his tomb,' identifying a specific location.
  • 往岁零雨 - Means 'last year during the rainy season,' setting a temporal context.
  • 屋舍倾圯 - Means 'the houses collapsed,' describing a state of ruin.
  • 佐领无力缮葺 - Means 'the assistant commander had no power to repair,' indicating a lack of resources or authority.
  • 老朽无容身处 - Means 'the old man had no place to stay,' describing a state of homelessness.
  • 故佣工于此 - Means 'so he worked here,' indicating a change in circumstances.
  • 聊以自活 - Means 'just to make a living,' expressing a basic survival motive.
  • 前日中元节 - Means 'last year during the Ghost Festival,' setting a temporal context.
  • 佐领殿墓 - Means 'the assistant commander performed a ritual at the tomb,' describing a specific action.
  • 犹焚船马数事 - Means 'still burned paper boats and horses,' indicating a traditional practice.
  • 第不知亡妻借马，何事何之耳 - Means 'but did not know why the deceased wife borrowed a horse and for what purpose,' expressing uncertainty.
  • 谭感叹久之 - Means 'Tan sighed for a long time,' describing an emotional reaction.
  • 乃解囊赠以青蚨五百 - Means 'then took out his purse and gave him five hundred coins,' indicating a generous act.
  • 俾具冥资 - Means 'to provide spiritual funds,' expressing a purpose.
  • 勿使魂馁 - Means 'so that their spirits would not be hungry,' expressing a wish.
  • 泣谢 - Means 'cried and thanked,' describing an emotional response.
  • 谭归后，不欲食言于鬼 - Means 'after Tan returned, he did not want to break his promise to the spirits,' expressing a commitment.
  • 亟备纸烟具二枚，烟一封 - Means 'quickly prepared two sets of paper and tobacco, and a bundle of tobacco,' indicating preparation.
  • 重至其墓，祝而焚之 - Means 'went back to the tomb, made a wish, and burned them,' describing a ritual.
  • 更访巴参领墓 - Means 'then visited Ba's assistant commander's tomb,' indicating a further action.
  • 果在直北数十武外 - Means 'indeed it was directly north, about several dozen steps away,' providing a precise location.
  • 松柏森郁 - Means 'the pines and cypresses were dense and lush,' describing the environment.
  • 有新碑可扪云 - Means 'there was a new stele that could be touched,' indicating a physical presence.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 中元节 - The Ghost Festival, a traditional Chinese festival for honoring the dead.
  • 冥资 - Spiritual funds, offerings made to the spirits of the deceased.
  • 纸烟具 - Paper and tobacco, used in traditional Chinese rituals.
  • 松柏 - Pine and cypress trees, often planted around tombs for their evergreen nature and symbolic significance.
  • 新碑 - A new stele, a stone monument often placed at tombs.

Proper Nouns

  • 谭 - Tan, the main character.
  • 郝四 - Hao Si, a character in the story.
  • 巴参领 - Ba, an assistant commander.
  • 中元节 - The Ghost Festival.
  • 冥资 - Spiritual funds.
  • 纸烟具 - Paper and tobacco.
  • 松柏 - Pine and cypress trees.
  • 新碑 - A new stele.

[412]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一饭之恩 - A phrase meaning 'the kindness of a single meal,' used to emphasize small acts of kindness.
  • 夜台魂馁 - A phrase meaning 'the spirits of the dead are hungry,' used to express the suffering of the deceased.
  • 泉下神悲 - A phrase meaning 'the gods in the netherworld are sorrowful,' used to express the sorrow of the spirits.
  • 倍可伤矣 - A phrase meaning 'it is even more pitiful,' used to emphasize the depth of sorrow.
  • 佣工野肆中 - A phrase meaning 'working in a remote tavern,' used to describe a humble occupation.
  • 暂谋糊口 - A phrase meaning 'temporarily seeking to make ends meet,' used to describe a precarious living situation.
  • 自顾不暇 - A phrase meaning 'too preoccupied with one's own affairs,' used to describe a lack of resources.
  • 妻子嗷嗷 - A phrase meaning 'children and wife are hungry and crying,' used to express the suffering of family members.
  • 犹待哺于地下 - A phrase meaning 'still waiting to be fed in the underworld,' used to describe the continued suffering of the deceased.
  • 鬼而贫也 - A phrase meaning 'the poor spirits,' used to describe the plight of the deceased.
  • 尚有阳世以为不时之需 - A phrase meaning 'still have the world of the living to rely on for occasional needs,' used to express a glimmer of hope.
  • 人而贫也 - A phrase meaning 'the poor people,' used to describe the plight of the living.
  • 其将告助于谁氏耶 - A phrase meaning 'to whom will they turn for help?' used to express a sense of desperation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 兰岩 - A person or place name, possibly a pseudonym or a specific location.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 无何 - A temporal adverb meaning 'soon after' or 'shortly,' indicating a brief passage of time.
  • 月升，值孟秋之半 - Describes the time as 'when the moon rises, at the middle of the first month of autumn,' emphasizing the seasonal context.
  • 炎暑未消 - Means 'the intense summer heat has not yet dissipated,' highlighting the lingering warmth of summer.
  • 梧叶乍飘 - Literally 'the paulownia leaves begin to flutter,' evoking a sense of autumn's arrival.
  • 蛩声四聚 - Refers to the sound of insects (grasshoppers or crickets) gathering around, symbolizing the onset of autumn.
  • 陆独步荒砌 - Means 'Lu walks alone on the desolate stone path,' suggesting solitude and reflection.
  • 寺门剥啄声，甚杂沓 - Describes the sound of someone knocking on the temple door, with '剥啄' being an onomatopoeic term for knocking, and '杂沓' indicating the sound is loud and chaotic.
  • 僧倒屣出应 - Means 'the monk rushes out to answer, even overturning his shoes,' showing the monk's eagerness or haste.
  • 褐衣三五辈 - Refers to 'three or five people in rough clothing,' likely peasants or commoners.
  • 将主人命，邀僧于山楼 - Means 'on behalf of the master, they invite the monk to the mountain pavilion,' indicating a formal invitation.
  • 践玩月之约者也 - Means 'to fulfill the moon-viewing appointment,' suggesting a social or cultural activity.
  • 僧诺之，随引扉相与西去 - Means 'the monk agrees and follows them as they open the door and go west together,' describing the monk's compliance and the group's departure.
  • 陆私念此僧，踪迹可疑久矣 - Means 'Lu privately thinks this monk has been suspicious for a long time,' indicating Lu's distrust.
  • 今盍试从其所之，观其所事 - Means 'now why not follow where he goes and observe what he does,' suggesting Lu's decision to investigate.
  • 足遣旅怀 - Means 'to dispel the loneliness of travel,' indicating the purpose of the investigation.
  • 倘有所见，亦可以助异日朋友相聚时，花前月下之谈资也 - Means 'if there is anything to see, it can also serve as material for conversation among friends in the future, under the flowers and moon,' suggesting the potential for future storytelling.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遂潛履其迹 - 遂 means 'then' or 'thereupon,' 潛履 means 'to stealthily follow,' and 其迹 means 'his/her/its tracks.' Describes the act of following someone's path in secret.
  • 迤逦行数里 - 迤逦 means 'winding' or 'twisting,' and 行数里 means 'to walk several miles.' Describes a winding journey over several miles.
  • 山路迍邅 - 山路 means 'mountain path,' and 迍邅 means 'difficult' or 'obstructed.' Describes a difficult or obstructed mountain path.
  • 半依峭壁 - 半依 means 'partially leaning on,' and 峭壁 means 'steep cliff.' Describes a structure that is partially leaning on a steep cliff.
  • 莅半深谭 - 莅 means 'overlooks' or 'faces,' and 半深谭 means 'a deep pool.' Describes a structure that overlooks a deep pool.
  • 阶砌倾危 - 阶砌 means 'steps' or 'stairs,' and 倾危 means 'sloping' or 'precarious.' Describes steps or stairs that are sloping or precarious.
  • 窗寮毁败 - 窗寮 means 'windows and small rooms,' and 毁败 means 'ruined' or 'decayed.' Describes windows and small rooms that are ruined or decayed.
  • 陆度不可登 - 陆度 means 'to cross by land,' and 不可登 means 'cannot be climbed.' Describes a path that cannot be crossed by land or climbed.
  • 适旁有古松 - 适 means 'happened to be' or 'just happened,' and 旁有古松 means 'there was an ancient pine tree nearby.' Describes a situation where an ancient pine tree was nearby.
  • 虬枝夭矫 - 虬枝 means 'twisted branches,' and 夭矫 means 'graceful' or 'elegant.' Describes twisted branches that are graceful or elegant.
  • 倚于巨石 - 倚 means 'leaning against,' and 巨石 means 'large rock.' Describes branches leaning against a large rock.
  • 因攀松踞石，平跳楼中 - 因 means 'therefore' or 'thus,' 攀松 means 'climbing the pine tree,' 踞石 means 'sitting on the rock,' and 平跳楼中 means 'jumping into the building.' Describes the action of climbing the pine tree, sitting on the rock, and then jumping into the building.
  • 色色洞晰 - 色色 means 'all sorts of,' and 洞晰 means 'clearly visible.' Describes all sorts of things being clearly visible.
  • 列双烛，设两筵 - 列双烛 means 'arranging two candles,' and 设两筵 means 'setting up two tables.' Describes the arrangement of two candles and two tables.
  • 长裾高履者三人 - 长裾 means 'long robes,' 高履 means 'high shoes,' and 三人 means 'three people.' Describes three people wearing long robes and high shoes.
  • 貌悉奇伟 - 貌 means 'appearance,' 悉 means 'all,' and 奇伟 means 'remarkable' or 'extraordinary.' Describes the appearance of all three people as remarkable or extraordinary.
  • 靓妆女子一人 - 靓妆 means 'beautifully dressed,' and 女子一人 means 'one woman.' Describes a beautifully dressed woman.
  • 绝美丽 - 绝 means 'extremely,' and 美丽 means 'beautiful.' Describes the woman as extremely beautiful.
  • 早一刻非不佳 - 早一刻 means 'a little earlier,' 非不佳 means 'not bad.' Describes the idea that a little earlier would not have been bad.
  • 奈何俗客在宾榻 - 奈何 means 'how can,' 俗客 means 'ordinary guest,' and 在宾榻 means 'on the guest's bed.' Describes the situation where an ordinary guest is on the guest's bed.
  • 故少煞风景 - 故 means 'therefore,' 少煞风景 means 'lessens the scenery' or 'spoils the view.' Describes the idea that the presence of the ordinary guest spoils the view.
  • 袁师知之乎 - 袁师 means 'teacher,' 知之乎 means 'do you know about it?' Asks if the teacher knows about something.
  • 郦三妹不矜细行 - 郦三妹 means 'the third sister,' 不矜细行 means 'does not care about small details.' Describes the sister as not caring about small details.
  • 竟遭薏苡之谤 - 竟遭 means 'ended up suffering,' 薏苡之谤 means 'the slander of the water chestnut.' Describes the sister ending up suffering from slander.
  • 乃翁督责过峻 - 乃翁 means 'your father,' 督责 means 'strict supervision,' and 过峻 means 'too severe.' Describes the father's strict supervision as too severe.
  • 三妹娇养惯 - 三妹 means 'the third sister,' 娇养惯 means 'used to being pampered.' Describes the third sister as used to being pampered.
  • 哭泣竟日 - 哭泣 means 'crying,' and 竟日 means 'all day.' Describes the sister crying all day.
  • 目尽肿 - 目 means 'eyes,' 尽肿 means 'completely swollen.' Describes the sister's eyes as completely swollen.
  • 今夕不识能赴约与否 - 今夕 means 'tonight,' 不识 means 'do not know,' 能赴约 means 'can keep the appointment,' and 与否 means 'or not.' Describes the uncertainty of whether the sister can keep the appointment tonight.
  • 如爽约 - 如 means 'if,' 爽约 means 'break the appointment.' Describes the situation where the sister breaks the appointment.
  • 则师之煞风景，未必如是之甚 - 则 means 'then,' 师之煞风景 means 'the teacher spoiling the view,' 未必 means 'not necessarily,' and 如是之甚 means 'as severe.' Describes the idea that the teacher spoiling the view is not necessarily as severe as the sister breaking the appointment.
  • 不然 - 不然 means 'otherwise' or 'not so.'
  • 郦三娘苟知袁师至 - 郦三娘 means 'the third sister,' 苟知 means 'if she knows,' and 袁师至 means 'the teacher has arrived.' Describes the situation where the sister knows the teacher has arrived.
  • 目肿即消 - 目肿 means 'eyes swollen,' 即消 means 'immediately disappear.' Describes the sister's eyes swelling immediately disappearing.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 何晏 - A historical figure from the Three Kingdoms period, known for his intelligence and beauty.

  • 薏苡之谤 - A reference to a historical incident where a person was falsely accused of carrying valuable items (water chestnuts) from a defeated enemy, leading to slander and punishment.
  • 乃翁 - A term of respect for a father, commonly used in classical Chinese literature.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 遂潜履其迹，迤逦行数里，山路迍邅 - This long sequence must be read as a continuous description of the journey.
  • 半依峭壁，莅半深谭，阶砌倾危，窗寮毁败 - This sequence describes the appearance of the building, with each clause providing a different aspect.
  • 陆度不可登，适旁有古松，虬枝夭矫，倚于巨石 - This sequence describes the surroundings and the method of entry into the building.
  • 因攀松踞石，平跳楼中 - This sequence describes the action of entering the building.
  • 色色洞晰 - This phrase summarizes the clarity of the scene inside the building.
  • 见楼中列双烛，设两筵，长裾高履者三人，貌悉奇伟，靓妆女子一人，绝美丽 - This sequence describes the people inside the building, with each clause providing a different detail.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 深山窮谷，何所不有？而此事尤奇 - Rhetorical question: 'What does not exist in deep mountains and remote valleys? Yet this matter is especially strange.'
  • 貌姣媚如好女子 - Metaphor: 'His appearance was as beautiful and charming as a good-looking woman.'
  • 見者无不啧啧而目送 - Idiomatic: 'Everyone who saw him would remark in astonishment and gaze after him.'
  • 啧啧 - Onomatopoeic expression indicating admiration or surprise.
  • 闽中俗尚龙阳 - '龙阳' is a classical reference to male homosexuality, from a story in the *Han Shu* about a man named Longyang admired by the King of Zhao.
  • 片肌寸体，未尝轻露于人 - Metaphor: 'Not a single inch of his body was ever lightly exposed to others.'
  • 税居城北余氏废园 - '税居' means 'to rent or live in a place temporarily' (tax here is not about tax but about renting). '废园' means 'an abandoned garden.'
  • 地幽僻，少人踪 - '幽僻' means 'remote and secluded.' '少人踪' means 'few people's footprints' or 'rarely visited.'
  • 濯足石上 - '濯足' is a classical expression meaning 'to wash one's feet.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 漏三下，術人炷香於爐，焚符於燭，教二童俯身從胯下反視幾下圭竇，問曰：‘何所見？’曰：‘開門矣。’ This long sequence must be read as a series of actions leading up to the ritual and the children's response.
  • 術人即噙水向壁上噀之，復亟問：‘何如？’童曰：‘梳頭矣。勻面矣。’ This sequence continues the ritual and the children's responses, indicating the progression of the ritual.
  • 既而曰：‘易履矣，著衣矣。’ This phrase indicates the next stages of the ritual.
  • 術人曰：‘然則可以出矣。’ This phrase marks the conclusion of the ritual.
  • 噙水三噀之，瞥見一女子立幾後，約長五尺許，衣大紅衫，拖素裙，眉目娟好，微笑作羞恥態。 This sequence describes the apparition, including her appearance and demeanor.
  • 術人嘱曰：‘太太在此，可為禮。’ This phrase instructs the children to show respect.
  • 童子力牽，女子力卻，相持良久。 This phrase describes a struggle between the children and the apparition.
  • 術人作急狀，止之曰：‘彼修仙人，性村野，且放手，我自有處。’ This phrase explains the reason for the struggle and the resolution.

  • 彼亦被锁，尚笑人耶 - "彼" means "he," "亦被锁" means "was also locked," "尚笑人耶" means "still laughed at others?" Together, it means "He was also locked, still laughed at others?"
  • 归，逢所知则告之 - "归" means "returned," "逢" means "met," "所知" means "those he knew," "则告之" means "and told them." Together, it means "Returned, met those he knew, and told them."

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 復相與歷院宇數重 - A phrase describing the act of passing through multiple courtyards or buildings, likely of a large estate or mansion. '復相與' implies a repeated or collective action of moving through.
  • 始達正寢 - '始' means 'only then' or 'finally'; '達' is 'arrive at'; '正寢' is the main or principal hall or chamber, often used for the main living or receiving area of a noble household.
  • 倚隱囊而坐 - '倚' is to lean or recline; '隱囊' is a type of cushion or reclining pillow used in ancient times for comfort. So this phrase describes someone reclining on a cushion.
  • 年約四十許 - '約' is 'approximately'; '四十許' is 'around forty years old.' This is a common way to express an approximate age in Classical Chinese.
  • 媒再拜 - '媒' is a matchmaker or go-between; '再拜' is a formal act of bowing twice, a sign of respect and submission.
  • 夫人命曳起賜坐 - '命曳起' is 'ordered to be pulled up' or 'ordered to rise'; '賜坐' is 'granted a seat,' a gesture of honor and hospitality.
  • 自展邦族 - '自展' is 'to introduce oneself' or 'to present'; '邦族' is 'family background' or 'clan.' This phrase is used to introduce the family lineage or status of a person.
  • 三豚兒已冠 - '豚兒' is a term of endearment for a son, possibly meaning 'little pig' as a term of affection; '已冠' means 'has reached the age of coming-of-age' (i.e., has undergone the guan ceremony, which marks adulthood in ancient China).
  • 未有佳婦 - '佳婦' is a well-matched or suitable wife, often referring to a bride of high quality or virtue.
  • 媪能為我攀一门楣 - '媪' is an old woman or a female servant; '攀一门楣' is an idiom meaning 'to secure a good match' or 'to find a suitable husband for one's son.' '门楣' literally means 'door lintel,' but metaphorically refers to the status of a family.
  • 必當重賞也 - '必當' is 'will certainly'; '重賞' is 'heavy reward' or 'a generous reward.'
  • 亭亭玉立，英妙绝伦 - '亭亭玉立' is a classical idiom describing someone (usually a woman) standing tall and graceful, like jade; '英妙绝伦' means 'exceedingly beautiful and outstanding.'
  • 遮莫老身减齿三十年，亦必拼死充作姬媵 - '遮莫' is an archaic particle used to emphasize a statement, similar to 'even if' or 'no matter how'; '老身' is a modest term for oneself, used by an older woman; '减齿' is 'lose ten years of age' or 'age ten years less'; '拼死' is 'to die for it' or 'to risk one’s life'; '充作姬媵' is 'to serve as a concubine or妾,' a low-status wife.
  • 阿谁有闺秀，肯不急设东床 - '阿谁' is an archaic way of asking 'who'; '闺秀' is a well-educated, virtuous woman; '东床' is a term for a groom, referencing the story of Wang Xizhi, whose daughter was married to a man who was found sleeping on the eastern bed (a sign of carelessness, but also of sincerity). This phrase is a rhetorical question: 'Who would not urgently arrange a match for a daughter?'
  • 左右闻者皆笑 - '左右' is 'those around' or 'the attendants'; '闻者' is 'those who heard'; '皆笑' is 'all laughed.'
  • 夫人亦解颜曰 - '解颜' is 'to relax one’s expression' or 'to smile.'
  • 无怪婆子起家，谈锋煞是犀利 - '无怪' is 'no wonder'; '婆子' is an old woman or servant; '起家' is 'to rise from a humble background' or 'to come from a low status'; '谈锋' is 'the sharpness of speech' or 'eloquence'; '煞是' is an intensifier, 'very'; '犀利' is 'sharp' or 'keen.'
  • 第老身谋聘者，为东偏薛参政女 - '第' is 'but' or 'however'; '谋聘者' is 'the one I am trying to match'; '东偏' is 'east wing' or 'east side,' possibly indicating a specific part of the estate or the location of the family; '薛参政' is 'the daughter of Minister Xue' (薛 is a surname, 参政 is a title, 'participating in government affairs' or 'minister'); '女' is 'daughter.'
  • 参政已捐馆 - '参政' is a title; '捐馆' is a euphemism for 'has passed away' or 'has left this world.'
  • 夫人牛氏，择婿甚苛，且多疑，往往垂成而悔 - '夫人牛氏' is 'Madam Niu,' the wife of the deceased minister; '择婿甚苛' is 'chooses a groom very strictly'; '多疑' is 'very suspicious'; '垂成而悔' is 'on the verge of being completed, but then regret it' – meaning she often backs out at the last moment.
  • 婆子自料能令此事必谐否 - '婆子' is the matchmaker; '自料' is 'to estimate oneself' or 'to think oneself capable'; '此事' is 'this matter'; '必谐' is 'certainly succeed' or 'certainly be arranged.'
  • 老身平生，不惯作模棱语 - '老身' is again used by the matchmaker; '平生' is 'throughout one’s life'; '不惯' is 'not accustomed'; '作模棱语' is 'to speak in vague or ambiguous terms.'
  • 凭三寸舌往说之，必有佳报 - '凭三寸舌' is a classical idiom meaning 'relying on one’s eloquence' or 'using only words'; '往说之' is 'to go and persuade her'; '必有佳报' is 'there will certainly be good news.'
  • 夫人喜，饮食之，便促其行 - '饮食之' is 'fed her' or 'treated her to food'; '促其行' is 'urged her to leave' or 'hurried her on her way.'
  • 媒请诘朝往 - '诘朝' is 'the next morning' or 'the following day.'
  • 事不宜迟，迟则中变矣 - '事不宜迟' is 'the matter should not be delayed'; '迟则中变矣' is 'if delayed, the situation may change in the middle.'
  • 媒不敢方命，夫人仍使为伴 - '不敢方命' is 'dared not disobey' or 'was not brave enough to refuse'; '方命' is 'to defy or disobey'; '仍使为伴' is 'still sent her along as a companion.'
  • 向东二里许，即至其处 - '向东二里许' is 'two li to the east approximately'; '即至其处' is 'then arrived at the place.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 媒 - A matchmaker or go-between, a common figure in traditional Chinese society who arranged marriages, especially for the upper classes.
  • 夫人 - A term for a married woman, often used for the wife of a noble or high-ranking official. Here, it refers to a woman of high social status.
  • 隐囊 - A type of reclining cushion or pillow used in ancient China, often used by scholars and officials to recline while reading or resting.
  • 三豚儿 - '豚兒' is a term of endearment for a son, possibly a nickname or affectionate term. '三' indicates that this is the third son.
  • 冠 - The guan ceremony, a coming-of-age rite for young men in ancient China, marking the transition to adulthood and the ability to marry.
  • 门楣 - A metaphor for family status or prestige. To '攀一门楣' is to secure a good match for one’s son, indicating that the family of the bride is of high status.
  • 姬媵 - A concubine or妾, a secondary wife of lower status than the principal wife.
  • 东床 - A reference to the story of Wang Xizhi, whose daughter was married to a man who was found sleeping on the eastern bed (a sign of carelessness, but also of sincerity). The term is used here to refer to a groom.
  • 参政 - A title meaning 'participating in government' or 'minister'

  • 老身已心许之矣 - Phrase meaning 'I have already given my heart to him' or 'I have already accepted him in my heart.'
  • 小女往省其舅妗 - Phrase meaning 'my daughter has gone to visit her in-laws' or 'my daughter is visiting her husband’s parents.'
  • 三日后甫得还 - Phrase meaning 'she will only return after three days.'
  • 为致声卢家亲母 - Phrase meaning 'please convey my message to the Lu family's mother' or 'please tell the Lu family's mother.'
  • 打点纳采 - Phrase meaning 'to prepare the betrothal gifts' or 'to arrange the betrothal ceremony.'
  • 约清明节后 - Phrase meaning 'around the time of the Qingming Festival' or 'after the Qingming Festival.'
  • 嫁奁可完 - Phrase meaning 'the dowry can be completed' or 'the wedding gifts can be arranged.'
  • 即归鱼轩 - Phrase meaning 'then she will return to the fish carriage' or 'then she will return to the groom’s family.'
  • 儿女皆各长成，无事耽阁，终非了局 - Phrase meaning 'both children are now grown, and there is no reason for delay; this cannot remain unresolved.'
  • 汝亦不必奔波往返 - Phrase meaning 'you need not go back and forth' or 'you need not make the trip.'
  • 年老路迂，劳劳碌碌，心所不忍 - Phrase meaning 'you are old, the journey is long, and it is too burdensome for my heart to bear.'
  • 且汝辛苦不辞者，徒以欲得酬谢礼物耳 - Phrase meaning 'and though you are willing to endure the hardship, it is only to receive the gifts of thanks.'
  • 老身即便相付，再来亦素手矣 - Phrase meaning 'I will give it to you right away; if you come again, I will have nothing to give.'
  • 乃取廿金赠之 - Phrase meaning 'she then gave twenty taels of silver to her.'
  • 媒且喜且拜，以谀之曰 - Phrase meaning 'the matchmaker was both happy and bowed, flattering her by saying...'
  • 端底大家不比小户，见理真，故作事快 - Phrase meaning 'Indeed, a great family is not like a small household; they see things clearly, so they act quickly.'
  • 牛大悦，命具酒，并款之 - Phrase meaning 'Niu was greatly pleased and ordered wine to be prepared, treating her hospitably.'
  • 果盘中有杖杜，甚甘美 - Phrase meaning 'In the fruit tray was a kind of fruit called 'zhangdu,' which was very sweet and delicious.'
  • 媒伺隙，以帕裹纳袖中 - Phrase meaning 'the matchmaker took the opportunity to wrap it in a handkerchief and put it into her sleeve.'
  • 饮数巡，谢酒辞行 - Phrase meaning 'After drinking several rounds, she thanked them for the wine and took her leave.'
  • 牛复叮咛，速就婚礼 - Phrase meaning 'Niu repeated her instructions, urging her to proceed with the wedding quickly.'
  • 媒敬诺 - Phrase meaning 'The matchmaker respectfully agreed.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 巨宅一区 - Refers to a large estate or mansion, common in classical Chinese literature to describe the homes of wealthy or aristocratic families.
  • 沤钉兽环 - Describes ornate door fittings typical of wealthy homes in classical Chinese society, with '沤钉' possibly referring to a type of ornamental nail or fastening, and '兽环' meaning animal-shaped door knockers.
  • 卢氏 - Refers to the Lu family, likely a prominent or wealthy family in the story, similar to the 'Luo family' in classical Chinese literature.
  • 媒 - Refers to a matchmaker, a common figure in classical Chinese society who facilitated marriage arrangements.
  • 纳采 - A traditional term for the first of the six wedding rituals in ancient Chinese marriage ceremonies, involving the presentation of betrothal gifts.
  • 清明节 - The Qingming Festival, a traditional Chinese holiday for honoring ancestors and visiting graves, often used as a reference point for timing in classical literature.
  • 嫁奁 - Refers to the dowry or wedding gifts brought by the bride to the groom’s family.
  • 鱼轩 - A term used to refer to the groom’s family or the groom’s home, often used metaphorically in classical texts.
  • 杖杜 - Likely refers to a type of fruit, possibly a misspelling or alternate name for '枣杜' (a type of jujube or date), or another fruit native to ancient China.
  • 廿金 - Refers to twenty taels of silver, a unit of currency in classical China.
  • 台阁品 - A term used to describe a person of high rank or noble bearing, often used in classical Chinese to describe someone of high social status.
  • 素手 - A classical term meaning 'empty-handed' or 'without anything to give,' often used to express a sense of helplessness or lack of resources.

  • 里许 - A measure of distance, roughly one Chinese 'li' (approximately 500 meters).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 閱數月 - After several months, indicating a passage of time.
  • 甲復晚散 - Jia returned late from a gathering.
  • 憶家中小兒出痘 - Remembering that his young child had smallpox.
  • 不可不歸 - Could not avoid returning home, emphasizing the necessity of returning.
  • 且陰計怪異之事偶或遭之，讵必常有 - Moreover, secretly calculating that strange events might occasionally occur, but they are not always present, expressing Jia's internal thoughts about the likelihood of unusual occurrences.
  • 骡导其故道仍如曩时 - The mule led the old path as before, describing the mule's familiar route.
  • 复往其处 - Returning to that place, indicating Jia's return to the same location.
  • 方回溯当日主况 - Just recalling the situation of that day, describing Jia's attempt to remember what happened.
  • 未已 - Not yet finished, indicating that Jia's recollection was still ongoing.
  • 远远灯光，随声又来，不一而足，益而三焉 - Far-off lights, accompanied by sounds, came one after another, increasing to three in number, describing the appearance of multiple lights and sounds.
  • 甲屡选怯 - Jia repeatedly chose to be timid, indicating Jia's fear and hesitation.
  • 不待骡惊，鞭入田中 - Without waiting for the mule to be startled, he lashed it into the field, describing Jia's impulsive action.
  • 此时黍稷已获 - At this time, the millet and grain had been harvested, indicating the season and the state of the fields.
  • 一望旷朗 - A vast and clear view, describing the open and clear landscape.
  • 须臾三物，鱼贯而至，形状犹昔，唯增一男 - In a moment, three beings appeared in a line, their shapes the same as before, but with an additional male, describing the sudden appearance of three figures.
  • 骡一见惊嘶 - The mule, upon seeing them, neighed in fright, describing the mule's reaction.
  • 三物截然而止，并立向甲啾啾作声，如小儿吹葱然 - The three beings stopped abruptly and stood together, making sounds like children blowing through chives, describing the eerie sounds made by the figures.
  • 甲不觉褫魄，昏坠骡下 - Jia was so frightened that he lost his soul and fainted from the mule, describing Jia's extreme fear and fainting.
  • 其父见骡之独逸以归也，知其子有变，即鸠合家人，操兵执炬，觅至所说僻径，遍索田中，良久始获，抢攘舁归，呼救半夜始苏，更述其怪，闻者罔不错愕 - His father, seeing the mule return alone, knew something was wrong with his son, and immediately gathered the family, armed with weapons and torches, to search the described secluded path. They searched the fields for a long time before finding Jia, who was carried back in a rush. He was called for help and woke up in the middle of the night, recounting the strange events. Those who heard him were all surprised and shocked, describing the father's actions and the community's reaction.
  • 其父延缁羽为禳，不复有效，越数日竟死 - His father invited monks and Taoists to perform rituals, but they were no longer effective. After several days, Jia died, describing the unsuccessful attempts to save Jia.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 小兒出痘 - Smallpox, a common and deadly disease in ancient times, often used in literature to convey themes of suffering and mortality.
  • 骡 - Mule, a common animal used for transportation in ancient China, often symbolizing endurance and reliability.
  • 黍稷 - Millet and grain, staple crops in ancient Chinese agriculture, often used in literature to describe rural settings and agricultural life.
  • 缁羽 - Monks and Taoists, religious figures who were often called upon to perform rituals and exorcisms in times of crisis.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 甲复晚散，忆家中小儿出痘，不可不归，且阴计怪异之事偶或遭之，讵必常有 - This sentence is elliptical and grammatically compact, but it can be read as: 'Jia returned late from a gathering, remembering that his young child had smallpox, and could not avoid returning home. Moreover, he secretly calculated that strange events might occasionally occur, but they are not always present.'
  • 骡导其故道仍如曩时，复往其处，方回溯当日主况，未已，远远灯光，随声又来，不一而足，益而三焉 - This sentence is also elliptical and can be read as: 'The mule led the old path as before, returning to that place. Jia was just recalling the situation of that day when far-off lights, accompanied by sounds, came one after another, increasing to three in number.'
  • 须臾三物，鱼贯而至，形状犹昔，唯增一男 - This sentence is a concise description of the sudden appearance of three figures, and can be read as: 'In a moment, three beings appeared in a line, their shapes the same as before, but with an additional male.'
  • 其父见骡之独逸以归也，知其子有变，即鸠合家人，操兵执炬，觅至所说僻径，遍索田中，良久始获，抢攘舁归，呼救半夜始苏，更述其怪，闻者罔不错愕 - This sentence is a complex sequence of actions and reactions, and can be read as: 'His father, seeing the mule return alone, knew something was wrong with his son, and immediately gathered the family, armed with weapons and torches, to search the described secluded path. They searched the fields for a long time before finding Jia, who was carried back in a rush. He was called for help and woke up in the middle of the night, recounting the strange events. Those who heard him were all surprised and shocked.'

Proper Nouns

  • 其父 - His father, referring to Jia's father.
  • 缁羽 - Monks and Taoists, religious figures who perform rituals.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 闲斋曰 - Classical formula meaning 'Idle Studio says' or 'Idle Studio states,' used to introduce personal commentary or reflection.
  • 予少游湟中 - Means 'I traveled to the region of湟 when I was young,' where 湟 refers to the湟水 (Kang River) area.
  • 临青海 - Means 'approached Qinghai Lake,' indicating the proximity to the lake.
  • 水之清如潇湘，深如彭泽 - A simile comparing the clarity of the water to that of the潇湘 (Xiao and Xiang rivers) and the depth to that of彭泽 (Pengze), both famous for their scenic beauty and depth.
  • 遥望波心烟一点 - Means 'looking far into the heart of the waves, a wisp of smoke appears,' describing a distant, almost ethereal sight.
  • 番人曰 - Means 'the locals said,' indicating that the following statement is attributed to the local people.
  • 龙驹岛也 - Means 'it is the Dragon Horse Island,' referring to an island in the lake.
  • 周回约千里 - Means 'circling about a thousand li (a unit of distance),' indicating the size of the island.
  • 其大倍于洞庭 - Means 'its size is double that of Dongting Lake,' emphasizing the vastness of the island.
  • 其后游闽，登夏门，观冥海 - Means 'later, I traveled to Fujian, climbed Xia Gate, and observed the冥海 (Minghai, a mythical sea),' indicating a subsequent journey.
  • 则青海犹盆池也 - Means 'then Qinghai Lake seemed like a small pond,' using a metaphor to describe the relative size of the lake.
  • 吁！水大亦何常之有 - An interjection expressing wonder, followed by a rhetorical question meaning 'How can the size of water be constant?' suggesting the variability of nature.
  • 所见大，则所过皆小；所见小，则所过皆大 - A philosophical statement meaning 'what one sees as large makes everything else seem small; what one sees as small makes everything else seem large,' reflecting on perception and perspective.
  • 覆杯水于堂坳之上，群蚁过之，如洪水之怀山襄陵也固宜 - A metaphorical statement meaning 'pouring a cup of water into a low place in the hall, the ants passing over it would seem like a flood surrounding mountains and hills,' illustrating the relativity of size and perception.

  • 甫入院门 - 甫 means 'just' or 'immediately,' and 入院门 means 'entering the courtyard gate.' Together, it conveys the idea of entering the courtyard right away.
  • 即见女倚栏而待 - 即见 means 'immediately see,' 女 means 'woman,' 倚栏 means 'leaning on the railing,' and 而待 means 'awaiting.' This phrase describes the woman leaning on the railing, waiting.
  • 把握极欢 - 把握 means 'hold' or 'grasp,' and 极欢 means 'extremely happy.' Together, it suggests a deep, joyful connection.
  • 布筵对酌 - 布筵 means 'laying out a table,' and 对酌 means 'drinking together.' This phrase describes setting up a table for drinking.
  • 各述倾慕 - 各述 means 'each recount,' and 倾慕 means 'admiration.' This phrase indicates that they each expressed their admiration for one another.
  • 从此依倚闺中，不离跬步 - 从此 means 'from this point on,' 依倚 means 'relying on,' 闺中 means 'in the inner chamber,' and 不离跬步 means 'not leaving a step.' This phrase suggests that they became inseparable, staying close to each other.
  • 忽春罗扬声户外也 - 忽 means 'suddenly,' 春罗 is a proper name, 扬声 means 'shouting,' and 户外 means 'outside the door.' This describes Spring Luo shouting from outside the door.
  • 主母归来矣 - 主母 means 'mistress' or 'mother-in-law,' and 归来矣 means 'has returned.' This phrase announces the mistress's return.
  • 男女受授不亲，促膝可乎 - 男女受授不亲 means 'men and women should not be too intimate,' and 促膝可乎 means 'can they sit close together?' This reflects a moral concern about the appropriateness of their closeness.
  • 田惶恐投地，愿甘责罚 - 田惶恐投地 means 'Tian was terrified and fell to the ground,' and 愿甘责罚 means 'willing to accept punishment.' This shows Tian's fear and submission.
  • 媪瞋目视女，女泪萦两颊，愧而不惧 - 媪瞋目视女 means 'the old woman glared at the woman,' 女泪萦两颊 means 'the woman's tears welled up in her eyes,' and 愧而不惧 means 'ashamed but not afraid.' This describes the emotional state of the woman.
  • 留亲下榻，竟成揖盗入门 - 留亲下榻 means 'inviting a relative to stay,' and 竟成揖盗入门 means 'it turned out to be like inviting a thief into the house.' This is a metaphorical expression suggesting that inviting someone in led to trouble.
  • 为是自家侄子，且似谨愿，非嬛薄者，故坦然付托，出外不疑 - 为是自家侄子 means 'because he is a family member,' 且似谨愿 means 'and seems to be diligent and sincere,' 非嬛薄者 means 'not frivolous or shallow,' and 故坦然付托，出外不疑 means 'so I entrusted him without hesitation, not doubting him.' This explains why the old woman trusted Tian.
  • 不意亲骨肉，才半月之久，何意草创便尔，禽处兽爱 - 不意亲骨肉 means 'unfortunately, even family members,' 才半月之久 means 'only for a month,' 何意草创便尔 means 'how could they have acted so hastily,' and 禽处兽爱 means 'like animals in their behavior.' This expresses the old woman's disappointment and shock.
  • 今之所谓少年老成者，尚可信哉 - 今之所谓少年老成者 means 'what we call mature young people today,' and 尚可信哉 means 'can they still be trusted?' This is a rhetorical question expressing doubt.
  • 第事已舛谬，侄之肉亦不足食 - 第事已舛谬 means 'but the matter has already gone wrong,' and 侄之肉亦不足食 means 'even my nephew's flesh is not worth eating.' This is a hyperbolic expression of frustration.
  • 今与侄约，领老身资本二千金，往山东贩货，须志如翁伯，勿为康乐 - 今与侄约 means 'now I make a deal with my nephew,' 领老身资本二千金 means 'take my capital of two thousand gold pieces,' 往山东贩货 means 'go to Shandong to trade goods,' 须志如翁伯 means 'must have the same determination as my father-in-law,' and 勿为康乐 means 'do not be lazy.' This sets the terms of the agreement.
  • 苟能获利三倍，即当以秀姑妻汝，否则无相见也 - 苟能获利三倍 means 'if you can make three times the profit,' 即当以秀姑妻汝 means 'I will marry my daughter Xiu Gu to you,' and 否则无相见也 means 'otherwise, we will never see each other again.' This outlines the conditions for the marriage.
  • 田崩角稽首，额为之坟 - 田崩角稽首 means 'Tian fell to the ground and bowed deeply,' and 额为之坟 means 'his forehead became a mound.' This describes Tian's deep bow of submission.

  • 甫入院门，即见女倚栏而待，把握极欢 - This sequence must be read as a continuous action: 'As soon as he entered the courtyard, he saw the woman leaning on the railing, waiting, and they were extremely happy.'
  • 从此依倚闺中，不离跬步 - This phrase must be read as a single unit: 'From this point on, they relied on each other in the inner chamber, never leaving a step apart.'
  • 忽春罗扬声户外也 - This phrase must be read as a single action: 'Suddenly, Spring Luo shouted from outside the door.'
  • 主母归来矣 - This phrase must be read as an announcement: 'The mistress has returned.'
  • 男女受授不亲，促膝可乎 - This phrase must be read as a question: 'Men and women should not be too intimate; can they sit close together?'
  • 田惶恐投地，愿甘责罚 - This phrase must be read as a single action: 'Tian was terrified and fell to the ground, willing to accept punishment.'
  • 媪瞋目视女，女泪萦两颊，愧而不惧 - This phrase must be read as a series of actions: 'The old woman glared at the woman, the woman's tears welled up in her eyes, and she was ashamed but not afraid.'
  • 留亲下榻，竟成揖盗入门 - This phrase must be read as a metaphor: 'Inviting a relative to stay turned out to be like inviting a thief into the house.'
  • 为是自家侄子，且似谨愿，非嬛薄者，故坦然付托，出外不疑 - This phrase must be read as an explanation: 'Because he is a family member, and seems to be diligent and sincere, not frivolous or shallow, so I entrusted him without hesitation, not doubting him.'
  • 不意亲骨肉，才半月之久，何意草创便尔，禽处兽爱 - This phrase must be read as an expression of disappointment: 'Unfortunately, even family members, only for a month, how could they have acted so hastily, like animals in their behavior.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 冢宅有棠梨树 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the ancestral house has a *tangli* tree.' *冢宅* refers to an ancestral home or family estate. *棠梨* is a type of tree (*Prunus pseudocerasus*, the Chinese cherry), often used in Classical Chinese poetry for its symbolic or aesthetic qualities.
  • 花已半卸 - Poetic expression meaning 'the flowers are half-fallen' or 'the blossoms are half-withered.' This suggests the tree is in the late spring or early summer, with its flowers beginning to fall.
  • 数武 - A measure of distance, approximately 10–20 paces (depending on the era). It is used to indicate a short distance from the main tree.
  • 小冢四五 - 'A few small tombs, about four or five in number.' *冢* refers to a burial mound or tomb. *小冢* is a small tomb, likely a family tomb or memorial.
  • 知为秋罗等瘗处也 - 'It is known to be the burial place of Qiu Luo and others.' *瘗* (yì) is a classical term for burial, often used in the context of interring the dead.
  • 拊膺大恸 - 'Clutching his chest and wailing in grief.' A vivid expression of deep sorrow, *拊膺* (fǔ yīng) literally means 'to press one’s chest.'
  • 始悟所遇，即其姑及表妹之鬼也 - 'He finally realized that those he had encountered were the spirits of his aunt and cousin.' *姑* is 'aunt' (paternal or maternal), and *表妹* is 'cousin' (paternal or maternal, depending on context).
  • 不肯负姑之恩、妹之情 - 'He would not betray the grace of his aunt or the affection of his cousin.' This is a moral commitment, emphasizing filial piety and loyalty.
  • 遂僦居村中 - 'He then rented a house in the village.' *僦居* is a classical term for renting or leasing a residence.
  • 鸠工百人营建墓道 - 'He gathered 100 workers to construct the tomb passage.' *鸠工* is a classical expression meaning 'to gather workers' or 'to employ laborers.'
  • 植松柏，筑垣墉 - 'Planted cypress and pine trees, and built walls and enclosures.' *松柏* are symbolic of longevity and virtue in Chinese culture. *垣墉* refers to walls or enclosures around a tomb.
  • 复想象旧宅，如式建宅一区 - 'He then imagined the old house and, in the same style, built a new house.' *如式* means 'in the same style' or 'according to the model.'
  • 买僮蓄婢即居焉 - 'He bought servants and concubines and lived there.' *僮* and *婢* are male and female servants, respectively.
  • 为墓道之主 - 'Became the master of the tomb passage.' This indicates a symbolic or spiritual role in caring for the tomb.
  • 终身誓不娶妇，但纳妾生子，以继田氏 - 'He vowed never to marry a wife, but to take concubines and have children to continue the *Tian* family.' *纳妾* is the act of taking a concubine. *继田氏* means to continue the *Tian* lineage.
  • 每逢节序，必厚奠恸哭而祭之 - 'Whenever a festival or holiday came, he would offer a generous sacrifice, wail in grief, and hold a memorial ceremony.' *节序* refers to seasonal festivals or holidays, *厚奠* is a generous offering, and *恸哭* is loud weeping.
  • 恩茂先有田数顷，隶顺德，时往征租 - 'En Maoxian had several acres of farmland under the jurisdiction of Shunde, and would occasionally go to collect the rent.' *隶顺德* means 'belonging to Shunde,' a district or county in Guangdong province.
  • 与田氏子相交，诚恂恂儒雅之美少年，而为隐君子者也 - 'He associated with the *Tian* family's son, who was truly a modest and refined young man, a recluse of virtue.' *恂恂儒雅* means modest and scholarly. *隐君子* is a classical term for a 'reclusive gentleman' or 'hidden virtuous man.'
  • 茂先下榻其家，因得吊女之墓焉 - 'Maoxian stayed at his home and thus had the opportunity to visit the girl’s tomb.' *下榻* means to stay or reside, often used in the context of visiting someone's home.
  • 其唱和之作，皆录归以示所亲，予因得寓目 - 'All of his poems written in response to each other were recorded and taken back to show to his relatives. I thus had the opportunity to read them.' *唱和之作* refers to poems written in response to each other, a common poetic practice in Classical Chinese.
  • 茂先有诗赠田，极温厚，得风人之旨 - 'Maoxian composed a poem in tribute to *Tian*, which was extremely warm and sincere, capturing the spirit of the *fengren*.' *风人* refers to the *Book of Songs* (Shijing) or the poetic tradition of the *feng* (folk) and *ya* (elegant) songs, which emphasized moral and emotional expression.
  • 具稿中，兹不载 - 'The manuscript is included in the collection, but it is not included here.' *具稿中* means 'included in the manuscript' or 'recorded in the draft.'

  • 日暮，澡体修容 - Classical expression meaning 'at dusk, he washed his body and groomed his appearance,' indicating preparation or ritual before an event.

  • 漏三下 - Classical time reference. '漏' refers to the water clock, and '三下' means the third strike, approximately 3 a.m.
  • 绰约如仙子 - Poetic description meaning 'graceful and delicate like a fairy,' used to describe a woman’s beauty.

  • 两月如一日也 - Phrase meaning 'two months passed as if it were one day,' emphasizing the continuity and regularity of the events.
  • 妾以诚告君，君其勿怖 - Formal statement from a woman addressing a man, meaning 'I tell you truthfully, please do not be afraid.'
  • 妾姚氏，父为明季太守 - 'I am Lady Yao, my father was a magistrate in the late Ming period,' a classical way of introducing oneself with one’s family status.
  • 曾居此署，颇事科敛 - 'He once served in this office and was known for collecting heavy taxes,' with '科敛' meaning tax collection, often in an oppressive sense.
  • 妾时少，年十八，以婚姻非时，憔悴而死 - 'When I was young, at eighteen, I died in sorrow because my marriage was not timely,' indicating a tragic early death due to social or familial circumstances.
  • 生时酷爱梨花 - 'In life, I loved the pear blossom,' a poetic reference to a personal preference.
  • 属纩时，嘱托老母，即瘗此园中梨树下 - 'At the time of my death (when the breath was taken from me), I entrusted my mother to bury me beneath the pear tree in this garden,' a classical expression for death and burial.
  • 幸勿以葑菲见弃也 - Polite request meaning 'Please do not reject me as you would a wild plant (葑菲),' referencing a classical poem to express humility and hope for acceptance.

  • 日暮 - Refers to the end of the day, a time often associated with solitude, reflection, or supernatural events in classical Chinese literature.

  • 妾 - A term used by women of lower or middle class to refer to themselves, indicating a modest and respectful tone.

  • 科敛 - A term for taxation or levies, often with a negative connotation, implying oppressive or excessive taxation.
  • 属纩 - A classical expression for the moment of death, when a silk thread was placed over the mouth to test for breath. It is a formal and poetic way of referring to death.
  • 瘗 - To bury, often used in classical texts to describe the act of interment, especially in a specific or symbolic location.
  • 梨花 - The pear blossom, a symbol of purity, beauty, and transience in Chinese poetry and art. The woman's love for pear blossoms may be symbolic of her character or fate.
  • 葑菲 - A reference to a line from the *Book of Songs* (《诗经》), used as a metaphor for a humble or unrefined person or thing. The phrase '勿以葑菲见弃' is a humble request not to be rejected or looked down upon.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sequence '井中黑暗如夜，泥深没踝，暗中摸索，无计可出，自拼必死' describes the protagonist's situation in the well, emphasizing the darkness, mud, and despair.

  • The sequence '已而有隙光透入，望如一线；即之，得一石门，力撼之，豁然开朗' describes the sudden change from a dark, confined space to a bright, open one.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 荀孺子 - Xun Ruzi; likely a nickname or honorific for a member of the Xun family, possibly a historical figure or fictional character. 'Ruzi' means 'child' or 'young man,' often used as a term of endearment or humility.

  • 迎親 - Wedding procession; a traditional Chinese wedding custom where the groom's family goes to the bride's family to bring her home.

  • 流五百里 - Exiled five hundred miles; a form of punishment where the person is sent to a distant location for labor.
  • 戍塵界關 - To guard the Dust Boundary Pass; likely a fictional or mythical frontier location, possibly symbolic of a remote or harsh place.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一日薄暮 - Classical expression meaning 'one day, at dusk,' setting the temporal and atmospheric scene.
  • 吳獨坐藤花下 - 'Wu sat alone beneath the wisteria flowers.' The phrase '獨坐' conveys solitude and introspection.
  • 東偏有屋數椽 - 'To the east, there was a house with several rooms.' '數椽' is a classical term for a small number of rooms or a modest house.
  • 隔以粉垣 - 'Separated by a white wall.' '粉垣' refers to a wall coated with white lime, common in classical Chinese architecture.
  • 久荒廢 - 'Long abandoned.' This phrase indicates that the house has been unused for a long time.
  • 忽聞其中有人絮語 - 'Suddenly, he heard someone speaking in hushed tones.' '絮語' suggests whispered or low conversation.
  • 谛听之 - 'Carefully listened to it.' A formal expression used to describe attentive listening.
  • 隐不可辨 - 'Faint and indistinct.' This indicates that the voices were unclear or hard to understand.
  • 大疑 - 'Great doubt.' This is a classical way of expressing confusion or suspicion.
  • 蹑足属耳于垣 - 'Crouched on tiptoe and pressed his ear to the wall.' '蹑足' is a classical term for moving quietly, and '属耳' means to listen closely.
  • 逾垣 - 'Climbed over the wall.' This verb is commonly used in classical Chinese to describe stealthily passing a wall.
  • 属目于窗 - 'Focused his eyes on the window.' This indicates he was watching through the window.
  • 貌极都美 - 'Their appearance was extremely beautiful.' '都美' is a classical expression for beauty or elegance.
  • 方巾阔服 - 'Square headpiece and wide robe.' This refers to traditional Han Chinese attire, with '方巾' being a type of headwear and '阔服' a wide robe, both typical of scholar-official dress.
  • 不类时装 - 'Not like modern fashion.' This indicates that their clothing is not contemporary.
  • 吴知其非人 - 'Wu knew they were not humans.' This is a clear statement that the characters are not ordinary people.
  • 亦不惊骇 - 'Nor was he alarmed.' This shows that although the situation is strange, Wu remains calm.
  • 第屏息以察其所事 - 'Only held his breath to observe what they were doing.' '第' here functions as 'only' or 'merely,' and '屏息' is a classical term for holding one's breath in silence.
  • 紫衣者手弄一玉指环 - 'The man in purple robes was playing with a jade ring on his finger.' This is a literal translation, but '弄' suggests a casual or meditative handling.
  • 且玩且叹曰 - 'While playing with it, he sighed and said.' This is a classical way of expressing a dual action: playing and speaking.
  • 非物之为美，美人之贻 - 'It is not the object that is beautiful, but the beauty that gave it to me.' This is a poetic and philosophical statement, emphasizing the value of the giver over the object.
  • 忆昔游酒泉，入云中，客晋阳，又居抱罕三年 - 'I recall when I traveled to Jiuquan, entered Yunzhong, stayed as a guest in Jinyang, and lived for three years in Baohan.' These are proper place names and should be translated as such.
  • 由临洮、皋兰一路，仍归于此 - 'From Lintao and Golan, along this road, I returned here.' This indicates a journey from these locations back to the current place.
  • 奇遇之多，指不胜偻 - 'The number of strange encounters was so great that I could not count them all.' '指不胜偻' is a classical expression meaning 'too many to count with fingers.'
  • 若今日所昵，未数数觏也 - 'But today's beloved one, I have not often met.' '昵' means 'beloved' or 'close to,' and '数数觏' means 'often met.'
  • 方我在临洮道上，与令叔謢霞公邂逅时 - 'When I was on the road to Lintao and met your uncle, the Lord Huoxia.' '邂逅' means a chance meeting.
  • 对坐河干，款言移晷 - 'We sat facing each other by the riverbank, and spoke at length for a long time.' '款言' means 'genuine conversation' or 'heartfelt speech,' and '移晷' is an old measure of time, roughly an hour or more.
  • 便云阿咸在　，不无所遇 - 'He said, 'Ah Xian is here, but I have not met nothing.' This is a bit unclear, possibly a transcription error or missing text. '阿咸' is a name, possibly a nickname or title.
  • 所惜道术浅薄，恐至磋跎 - 'What is regrettable is that my Daoist knowledge is shallow, and I fear I will be wasted.' '道术' refers to Daoist practice or knowledge, and '磋跎' means to waste or squander one's time.
  • 深萦怀抱 - 'Deeply troubled my heart.' This is a poetic way of expressing inner turmoil or concern.
  • 彼时我慰之曰 - 'At that time, I consoled him, saying...' This sets up a direct quotation from the speaker.
  • 季锢闭已久，内照晶莹 - 'The younger one has been sealed for a long time, yet his inner light is clear and bright.' '季' likely refers to a younger brother or younger sibling, and '内照晶莹' is a metaphor for inner clarity or spiritual radiance.

  • 何谓也？ - Simple question: 'What do you mean?'

  • 不谓公肆行无忌，迷昧殊深，不过今秋，将复化为虎矣 - Warning: 'I did not expect you to act without restraint, to be so deeply confused. By no later than this autumn, you will once again transform into a tiger.'
  • 然则奈何？ - Question expressing concern: 'Then what can be done?'
  • 无他术，静气平心，勉为善事，可以挽之 - Moral prescription: 'There is no other method—calm your mind, be at peace, and strive to do good deeds, and you may still avert it.'
  • 更赠公良药一刀圭，服之必效，勿蔑视也 - Final instruction: 'I will also give you a fine medicine, a single pill. Take it and it will surely work—do not look down upon it.'

  • 更为留连一饷 - Means 'spent some more time lingering.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • The Lantern Festival (上元节) is celebrated on the 15th day of the first lunar month, a major traditional Chinese festival.
  • The guards (金吾) not enforcing strict curfews or restrictions (金吾不禁) allows for more freedom and celebration during festivals.
  • 'Hu lu' (呼卢) is a traditional Chinese dice game, often played during festivals and social gatherings.
  • The Chinese night is divided into five watches, and the fourth watch (四更) corresponds to the hours of 3-5 AM.
  • Xi'an Gate (西安门) is a historical gate in Beijing, part of the city's ancient walls.
  • A teahouse (茶铺) is a common social gathering place in traditional Chinese culture.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The parallel structure '一念之善，虎可为人；玩忽旋生，人而为虎' compares the transformation of a tiger into a human and vice versa, emphasizing the thin line between virtue and vice.
  • The clause '此圣狂之间，在于几希也' explains that the difference between sainthood and madness lies in the subtlest of distinctions.
  • The phrase '虚亦非寻常兽也' emphasizes that the tiger is not an ordinary beast, suggesting its extraordinary nature.
  • The complex sentence '琰慷慨豪爽，故得变为虎，若世之险邪庸碌辈，恐欲变犬而亦不可得为守夜者矣，敢望成虎耶' contrasts the transformation of a generous and bold person into a tiger with the inability of ordinary, wicked, and mediocre people to even become watchmen, let alone tigers.
  • The introduction '傻白太监白某，面白，人称傻白' provides background information about the eunuch Bai, describing him as foolish and pale.
  • The phrase '年四十余矣' indicates that Bai is in his forties.
  • The long sentence '间尝为余言，其十六岁时，值上元节，金吾不禁，灯月交辉，从其叔之西城外祖母家，与诸姊妹兄弟呼卢半夜' describes Bai's experience during the Lantern Festival, including the relaxed atmosphere and his participation in a dice game.
  • The clause '四更后，始告归' indicates that Bai returned home after the fourth watch of the night.
  • The sentence '至半途，忽忆表妹所赠升官图一纸，骰子六枚，忘未携得，欲返取之' describes Bai's realization that he forgot to take the promotion map and dice, leading him to return to retrieve them.
  • The clause '叔不耐往还，约在西安门茶铺中坐候' explains that Bai's uncle was not patient with the back-and-forth and agreed to meet at a teahouse in Xi'an Gate.
  • The final sentence '白独返外家，取得二事，更为留连一饷，然后行' describes Bai's return to his maternal home, retrieval of the items, and additional time spent lingering before continuing his journey.

Proper Nouns

  • 上元节 - The Lantern Festival.
  • 金吾 - The guards or officials responsible for maintaining order.
  • 西安门 - A historical gate in Beijing.
  • 茶铺 - A teahouse.
  • 傻白太监白某 - The eunuch named Bai.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 荼毒子女 - Literally means 'to torment children,' referring to the cruel treatment of one's own children.
  • 终罹惨报 - Means 'ultimately suffer a terrible retribution,' indicating that one will face severe consequences for their actions.
  • 天心岂或爽哉 - A rhetorical question meaning 'Could the will of heaven possibly be mistaken?' This phrase affirms the inevitability and justice of divine retribution.
  • 铁公鸡 - A colloquial term for someone who is extremely stingy, literally 'iron rooster.'
  • 拥资数十万 - Means 'possessing assets worth tens of thousands,' indicating great wealth.
  • 性极悭吝 - Means 'having an extremely miserly nature.'
  • 居积取赢 - Refers to hoarding goods to make a profit, a common practice in mercantile activities.
  • 持筹会计 - Means 'manipulating counting rods for accounting,' a reference to meticulous financial management.
  • 日不暇给 - Means 'too busy to handle everything,' indicating a state of being overwhelmed by work.
  • 敝衣破帽 - Literally means 'tattered clothes and torn hats,' used to describe a person appearing very poor.
  • 向亲故作贫窭状 - Means 'pretending to be poor in front of relatives and acquaintances,' indicating a facade of poverty.
  • 老小数十口 - Means 'a family of several dozen people,' including both adults and children.
  • 日市肉半斤、菜数斤 - Means 'buying half a pound of meat and several pounds of vegetables daily,' describing a very modest diet.
  • 饭脱粟 - Means 'eating coarse millet,' a very simple and frugal diet.
  • 皆取给于一灶 - Means 'all getting their food from one stove,' indicating a shared meal.
  • 早餐恒午饭，晚餐恒夜食 - Means 'having breakfast for lunch and dinner for supper,' indicating irregular meal times.
  • 不设茶酒 - Means 'not serving tea or alcohol,' indicating a lack of hospitality.
  • 终年不宴客 - Means 'never hosting banquets throughout the year,' indicating a lack of social gatherings.
  • 虽骨肉至亲 - Means 'even close family members,' indicating a lack of generosity even towards the closest relatives.
  • 未尝见其匕箸是何形状 - Means 'never seeing what his chopsticks look like,' indicating a lack of familiarity with the utensils.
  • 翁亦不知款客作何周旋 - Means 'the old man also doesn't know how to entertain guests,' indicating a lack of social skills.
  • 往往见招于人 - Means 'often invited by others,' indicating that despite his stinginess, he is still invited to social events.
  • 歌筵舞席 - Means 'banquets with singing and dancing,' indicating lively social gatherings.
  • 颇极欢洽 - Means 'very enjoyable,' indicating that he enjoys these social events.
  • 又似毫不知生人之乐者 - Means 'yet seems unaware of the joy of socializing,' indicating a contradiction between his behavior and enjoyment.
  • 乡人号之为铁公鸡 - Means 'the villagers call him an iron rooster,' indicating the nickname given to him due to his stinginess.
  • 谓一毛不拔也 - Means 'meaning he won't part with even a single hair,' indicating extreme stinginess.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 荼毒子女 - A phrase that reflects the moral judgment of causing harm to one's own children, which is a serious offense in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 终罹惨报 - Reflects the belief in divine retribution and karma, a common theme in Chinese philosophy and religion.
  • 天心 - Refers to the will of heaven, a concept central to traditional Chinese thought, emphasizing the idea that the universe is governed by a moral order.
  • 铁公鸡 - A colloquial term that has been used for centuries to describe extremely stingy people, reflecting the cultural disdain for greed and miserliness.
  • 拥资数十万 - Indicates the level of wealth in ancient China, where having tens of thousands of assets was considered significant.
  • 性极悭吝 - Describes a personality trait that is highly disapproved of in traditional Chinese culture, where generosity and benevolence are valued.
  • 居积取赢 - Reflects the economic practices of the time, where hoarding goods to make a profit was a common strategy.
  • 持筹会计 - Refers to the use of counting rods, a traditional method of accounting in ancient China.
  • 日不暇给 - Describes a state of being overwhelmed by work, a common experience in traditional Chinese society.
  • 敝衣破帽 - Used to describe a person pretending to be poor, reflecting the social norms and expectations of modesty.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '既归，以白其母，母叹且泣曰' introduces the return, the act of informing the mother, and the mother's emotional response.
  • The rhetorical question '以汝父素行，又何事不屑为，神佛岂欺人哉' emphasizes the mother's belief in her husband's character and the impossibility of divine deception.
  • The clause '嗣此戒律愈严，女亦信心奉佛焉' links the increased strictness of rules to the daughter's devotion to Buddhism.
  • The sentence '其子年虽少，颇有父风，乡人称其克肖' describes the son's resemblance to his father and the villagers' recognition of this similarity.
  • The clause '屡梗母教，母甚忧之' highlights the son's resistance to his mother's teachings and the resulting concern.
  • The sentence '一日，有亲戚归自京师者，其子往候之' introduces a visit to a returning relative and sets the stage for the conversation.
  • The question '话及京师人物众矣，究竟何等人最乐' prompts the relative to discuss the happiest people in the capital.
  • The response '乐者甚伙，要皆高不可下耳' acknowledges the many happy people but emphasizes their unattainable status.
  • The statement '尔我今生断不能及' reinforces the idea that the speaker and the listener cannot match the status of the happy people.
  • The clause '唯一等人，极可歆羡，盖太监也' identifies eunuchs as the most enviable group.
  • The question '刑余之人，有何可乐' challenges the idea of happiness among eunuchs.
  • The statement '汝但知其人道已绝，必乏乐趣' reflects a common belief about the lack of human nature and pleasure among eunuchs.
  • The clause '而不知其可乐之处甚多' introduces the idea that eunuchs have many sources of joy.
  • The phrase '试为子偻指注之' suggests a detailed explanation will follow.
  • The sentence '夫王公至贵者也，然望天子之居，不翅天上' contrasts the high status of nobles with the even higher status of the emperor.
  • The clause '彼以阉故，得出入不禁，一乐也' explains one of the joys of being a eunuch.

  • 声震如雷 - The sound is as loud as thunder.
  • 白衣白冠，手持白幡 - Describes the giant's appearance, wearing white clothes and a white hat, holding a white banner.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 兰岩 - Likely a pseudonym or name of the author or a commentator.
  • 应城王家口 - A specific location, possibly a village or a place name.
  • 观音咒 - The Guanyin mantra, a Buddhist prayer or chant.
  • 不食牛肉 - A dietary restriction, possibly related to religious or cultural beliefs.
  • 暴死 - Sudden death, often associated with supernatural causes in traditional Chinese beliefs.

Proper Nouns

  • 兰岩 - The author or commentator.
  • 应城王家口 - The location where the giant appeared.
  • 观音咒 - The Guanyin mantra.
  • 不食牛肉 - A dietary restriction.
